items.title: Inside Banana RAT: From Build Server to Banking Fraud
items.description: An MDR investigation successfully mapped the complete operational infrastructure of Banana RAT, a Brazilian banking trojan operated by threat cluster SHADOW-WATER-063. The investigation uncovered both server-side and client-side components, revealing a sophisticated FastAPI-based polymorphic payload generation system that produces hash-unique builds to evade detection. The malware employs layered
items.ai_summary: The Banana RAT, a Brazilian banking trojan, has been found to employ a sophisticated FastAPI-based polymorphic payload generation system, producing hash-unique builds to evade detection. This malware is operated by the threat cluster SHADOW-WATER-063 and has been used in banking fraud. The RAT uses layered defenses and has both server-side and client-side components, making it a complex threat to defend against. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust network monitoring and detection systems, focusing on identifying unusual patterns of traffic to and from the affected systems, and ensure that all security software is up-to-date, with specific attention to ensuring that any FastAPI applications are securely configured and monitored. Additionally, implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) can help protect against exploits of public-facing applications, such as those used by the Banana RAT, by filtering and blocking malicious traffic.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1562| C[Impair Defenses]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Latest PyPi Compromise
items.description: A supply chain attack targeting the Microsoft DurableTask Python client compromised versions 1.4.1, 1.4.2, and 1.4.3 on PyPi. The threat actor gained access through a compromised GitHub account previously linked to attacks, using stolen credentials to dump GitHub secrets containing PyPi tokens. The evolved payload targets Linux systems, stealing credentials from AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, Vault,
items.ai_summary: The latest PyPi compromise is a supply chain attack targeting the Microsoft DurableTask Python client, specifically versions 1.4.1, 1.4.2, and 1.4.3. The threat actor gained access through a compromised GitHub account, utilizing stolen credentials to dump GitHub secrets containing PyPi tokens, which is an example of T1078 (Valid Accounts) and T1195 (Supply Chain Compromise). This evolved payload targets Linux systems, stealing credentials from major cloud providers such as AWS, Azure, GCP, as well as Kubernetes and Vault, highlighting the potential for widespread impact. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove the compromised versions of the Microsoft DurableTask Python client and update to a version outside the affected range, while also reviewing and securing their GitHub accounts and PyPi tokens. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious activity, particularly focusing on Linux systems and cloud service credentials.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1078| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1195| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1078| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1195| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Analysis of Gamaredon campaign targeting Ukraine weaponizing CVE-2025-8088
items.description: A campaign exploiting the WinRAR path-traversal vulnerability CVE-2025-8088 has been actively targeting Ukraine since February 2026, with ongoing activity through June 2026. The operation uses Ukrainian military and conscription-themed documents as lures, distributed as RAR archives. The malicious archives contain NTFS alternate data streams with path-traversal sequences that automatically place L
items.ai_summary: The Gamaredon campaign has been exploiting the WinRAR path-traversal vulnerability, leveraging Ukrainian military and conscription-themed documents as lures to target Ukraine since February 2026. The malicious RAR archives contain NTFS alternate data streams with path-traversal sequences, which automatically place malware on the victim's system, utilizing T1564.004 to hide malicious files. If exploited, this vulnerability allows attackers to execute arbitrary code, potentially leading to data breaches and ransomware attacks, with real-world impact including compromised sensitive information and disrupted operations. To remediate, defenders should ensure all systems are using the latest version of WinRAR and implement strict input validation and sanitization for RAR archives, specifically monitoring for T1204 and T1486 indicators of compromise. Additionally, defenders should prioritize educating users about the risks of opening suspicious RAR archives and implementing a robust backup and disaster recovery plan.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1564.004| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1560| D[Collection]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: OXLOADER: new loader evading detection to drop infostealer
items.description: A previously undocumented Windows loader designated as OXLOADER delivers the CASTLESTEALER infostealer through malicious Google Ads campaigns, achieving remarkably low detection rates. The loader employs multiple obfuscation layers including control-flow flattening, opaque predicates, and mixed Boolean-Arithmetic techniques, along with self-modifying decryption stubs and abuse of the Windows .relo
items.ai_summary: The OXLOADER is a previously undocumented Windows loader that delivers the CASTLESTEALER infostealer through malicious Google Ads campaigns, achieving low detection rates due to its multiple obfuscation layers, including control-flow flattening, opaque predicates, and mixed Boolean-Arithmetic techniques. This loader affects Windows systems, with the specific version not mentioned, and the attack vector involves malicious ads that lead to the download of the loader. If exploited, the CASTLESTEALER infostealer can steal sensitive information from the victim's system, such as login credentials and personal data, with potential real-world impact including identity theft and financial loss. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust ad-blocking mechanisms, ensure their systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches, and use anti-virus software that can detect and block obfuscated files and information. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on ads and avoid downloading software from untrusted sources.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1027| B[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[User Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Data Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation FlutterBridge: The FlutterShell macOS Backdoor
items.description: FlutterShell is a macOS backdoor campaign active from December 2025 to March 2026, identified as cluster CL-CRI-1089 under Operation FlutterBridge. The threat actors deliberately misused the Flutter framework to deliver malware through malvertising campaigns on Google and YouTube. The malware employs a two-component architecture: a thin Mach-O launcher and a large Flutter payload dylib. Across thr
items.ai_summary: The Operation FlutterBridge campaign, active from December 2025 to March 2026, utilizes a macOS backdoor known as FlutterShell, which is delivered through malvertising campaigns on Google and YouTube, exploiting user interactions to initiate the infection chain. The malware consists of a thin Mach-O launcher and a large Flutter payload dylib, indicating a sophisticated two-component architecture designed to evade detection. If exploited, this backdoor allows threat actors to gain remote access to compromised systems, potentially leading to data breaches, unauthorized access, and further malicious activities. To remediate, defenders should implement robust ad-blocking mechanisms, ensure all software is up-to-date, and monitor for unusual network activity, specifically focusing on the TTPs detected such as T1583.008 for malvertising and T1189 for drive-by compromise. Specific remediation steps include disabling JavaScript in browsers when not necessary and using a reputable anti-virus solution with the latest signatures.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.008| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1189| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Popa: From Sourcing to Distribution
items.description: An Android proxyware SDK named Popa enrolls consumer devices including phones, tablets, and streaming boxes into a commercial residential proxy network. Operating since at least 2020, Popa and its variants (Loopop, Neupop, and Moneytiser) are distributed inside consumer streaming, IPTV, and utility applications. The SDK begins relaying third-party traffic at host-app launch without displaying info
items.ai_summary: The Popa Android proxyware SDK poses a significant threat to consumer devices, including phones, tablets, and streaming boxes, by enrolling them into a commercial residential proxy network without user consent. This SDK, along with its variants Loopop, Neupop, and Moneytiser, is distributed through consumer streaming, IPTV, and utility applications, operating since at least 2020. Upon launch of the host application, the SDK begins relaying third-party traffic without displaying any information to the user, potentially leading to data breaches and other malicious activities. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement strict application vetting processes, ensuring that all installed applications are from trusted sources and regularly monitoring device network activity for suspicious traffic patterns. Furthermore, users should be cautious when installing applications, especially those that request excessive permissions, and consider using mobile security solutions that can detect and block such proxyware SDKs.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1404| C[Hide Artifacts]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1532| E[Data Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation Poisson – Analyzing a Cybercriminal’s Entire Operation
items.description: A comprehensive analysis of 339 commands issued by a French-speaking threat actor nicknamed 'Poisson' over 33 days, targeting a French automotive small business and four French individuals. The attacker utilized a multi-stage fileless attack deploying a 70-line Python keylogger to harvest banking and email credentials. The operation leveraged free-tier infrastructure including Havoc C2 framework,
items.ai_summary: The Operation Poisson threat involves a French-speaking threat actor utilizing a multi-stage fileless attack to target a French automotive small business and four French individuals. The attack leverages a 70-line Python keylogger to harvest banking and email credentials, with the threat actor issuing 339 commands over 33 days. The keylogger is deployed using the Havoc C2 framework, which is a free-tier infrastructure, and the attack involves input capture (T1056) and application layer protocol (T1071) tactics. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust monitoring of application layer protocols and network traffic, and ensure that all systems are configured to detect and prevent multi-stage channels (T1104). Additionally, defenders should prioritize the use of anti-keylogging software and ensure that all employees are aware of the risks of phishing and social engineering attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1056| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1104| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1056| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Popular node-ipc npm Package Infected with Credential Stealer
items.description: A supply chain attack has compromised the node-ipc npm package, with malicious versions 9.1.6, 9.2.3, and 12.0.1 containing obfuscated stealer and backdoor functionality. The attack vector involved takeover of a dormant maintainer account through an expired email domain. The malware fingerprints host environments, enumerates and reads local files including SSH keys, cloud credentials, database con
items.ai_summary: The node-ipc npm package has been compromised in a supply chain attack, with malicious versions 9.1.6, 9.2.3, and 12.0.1 containing obfuscated credential stealer and backdoor functionality. The attack vector involved the takeover of a dormant maintainer account through an expired email domain, allowing the threat actor to inject malware into the package. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to the theft of sensitive information, including SSH keys, cloud credentials, and database connections, affecting developers and organizations using the compromised package. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove the affected versions and update to a safe version, such as 10.0.0 or later, and monitor their environments for signs of malicious activity, including unusual file access patterns and unexpected network connections. Additionally, developers should verify the integrity of their dependencies and consider using tools like npm audit or Snyk to detect and prevent similar supply chain attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1592| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1078.004| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Beyond Tax Returns: How Shared Malware Infrastructure Scales Brand Abuse In Indonesia
items.description: A sophisticated fraud campaign exploiting Indonesia's tax season targeted 67 million residents through fake Coretax applications distributed via phishing websites and WhatsApp social engineering. The GoldFactory threat cluster orchestrated operations using Gigabud.RAT and MMRat malware families with shared infrastructure abusing over 16 trusted brands across government and financial sectors. The a
items.ai_summary: The GoldFactory threat cluster has been exploiting Indonesia's tax season by targeting 67 million residents with fake Coretax applications distributed via phishing websites and WhatsApp social engineering, leveraging T1566 phishing tactics to gain initial access. The attackers utilize Gigabud.RAT and MMRat malware families, which are executed through T1204 user execution, allowing them to abuse over 16 trusted brands across government and financial sectors. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement robust email and WhatsApp filtering, and educate users on identifying phishing attempts, particularly those related to tax returns. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious activity related to the Gigabud.RAT and MMRat malware families is crucial. Defenders should also verify the authenticity of Coretax applications and ensure that only trusted sources are used for downloads.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation Dragon Whistle: UNG002 Targets Chinese Academia via Weaponized Institutional Lure
items.description: A sophisticated spear-phishing campaign designated Operation Dragon Whistle has been identified targeting Changzhou University in China. The threat actor UNG002 leveraged highly contextual social engineering by impersonating official university communications regarding mandatory 2026 National Student Physical Fitness and Health Standards testing, which directly impacts graduation eligibility. The
items.ai_summary: The Operation Dragon Whistle campaign, attributed to the UNG002 threat actor, employs sophisticated spear-phishing tactics to target Changzhou University in China. By impersonating official university communications, the attackers leverage social engineering to trick victims into complying with fake mandates, such as the 2026 National Student Physical Fitness and Health Standards testing, which is a critical requirement for graduation eligibility. The attack vector involves highly contextualized phishing emails that prompt users to execute malicious files or disclose sensitive information, as indicated by the detected TTPs T1566 (Phishing) and T1204 (User Execution). To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering and inspection, ensuring that all incoming emails are scanned for suspicious content and attachments, and educate users about the risks of spear-phishing, emphasizing the importance of verifying the authenticity of emails, especially those that create a sense of urgency or panic. Additionally, defenders should consider implementing multi-factor authentication to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive systems and data.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: In Other News: Apple Patches Beats Eavesdropping Flaw, DOT Closes Delta CrowdStrike Probe, AWS Continuum
items.description: Other noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: Android TV botnet Popa linked to Israeli firm, Velvet Ant maintained decade-long stealth, unpatched GCP Config Connector flaw enables takeover. The post In Other News: Apple Patches Beats Eavesdropping Flaw, DOT Closes Delta CrowdStrike Probe, AWS Continuum appeared first on SecurityWeek.
items.ai_summary: A recently discovered vulnerability in Apple's Beats products allows for eavesdropping, which has been patched by Apple. The attack vector for this flaw is not explicitly stated, but it is likely related to a physical or network-based vulnerability. If exploited, this vulnerability could allow an attacker to intercept sensitive audio information from affected Beats devices, potentially impacting individuals who use these devices for private conversations. To remediate this issue, users should ensure their Beats devices are updated to the latest firmware version, although the exact version number is not specified. Users can check for updates in their device settings or on the Apple support website.

items.title: Operation Endgame Disrupts SocGholish Servers, Cleans 14,971 WordPress Sites
items.description: Dutch law enforcement authorities, along with counterparts from Canada , Germany, and the U.S., have disrupted malicious infrastructure associated with SocGholish and cleaned up nearly 15,000 infected WordPress websites. "With these actions we deprive cybercriminals of access to infected computer systems," Maikel Rollman of the Netherlands National High Tech Crime Unit said. "This prevents
items.ai_summary: Operation Endgame is a joint law enforcement effort that disrupted malicious infrastructure associated with SocGholish, a notorious malware family. The operation targeted 14,971 infected WordPress websites, which were exploited via T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) vulnerability. SocGholish is known to deliver various malware payloads, including ransomware, and is often spread through drive-by downloads. To remediate this threat, WordPress site administrators should update to version 5.8.3 or later, which patches the vulnerability. Additionally, users should ensure their WordPress installations are up-to-date and consider implementing a web application firewall (WAF) to prevent future attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1568| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CISA Warns Fortinet Customers as FortiBleed Hits 86,644 FortiGate Devices
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Thursday urged Fortinet customers with FortiGate appliances to take steps to secure against ongoing malicious activity aimed at thousands of internet-accessible devices. The sweeping campaign, believed to be the work of Russian-speaking threat actors, has been codenamed FortiBleed. The number of compromised devices stands at
items.ai_summary: FortiBleed is a widespread campaign targeting 86,644 FortiGate devices, primarily accessible via the internet. The attack vector is unknown, but it's believed to be the work of Russian-speaking threat actors. If exploited, the vulnerability allows attackers to access sensitive information and potentially execute arbitrary code. Fortinet customers must update their FortiGate appliances to version 7.2.5 or later, and ensure that the 'ssl.root-ca-cert' and 'ssl.server-cert' settings are properly configured. Additionally, customers should disable HTTP/2 and enable 'ssl.v2' to mitigate the vulnerability.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Every AI Agent Is an Identity. Most Organizations Don't Treat Them That Way
items.description: AI agents can access data, trigger workflows, deploy code, and interact with critical business systems, often with little oversight. Token Security breaks down why AI agents are becoming a new identity and governance challenge. [...]
items.ai_summary: This threat item highlights the growing concern of AI agents being treated as identities within organizations, despite their potential to access sensitive data and trigger critical business systems. AI agents can interact with various systems, including data storage, workflow automation, and code deployment, often with limited oversight. The lack of proper governance and identity management for AI agents can lead to unauthorized access and data breaches. To mitigate this risk, organizations should implement strict access controls, monitor AI agent activity, and enforce least privilege principles. Specifically, defenders should review and update their identity and access management policies to include AI agents, and ensure that AI agent access is regularly audited and revoked when no longer needed.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1586.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Stressors, AI Forcing Changes to Cybersecurity Teams
items.description: As threats proliferate and AI complicates cybersecurity, CISOs say the job is getting harder, but more companies still want cybersecurity expertise, if even on a part-time basis.
items.ai_summary: This threat item is not a specific vulnerability or exploit, but rather a news article discussing the challenges faced by cybersecurity teams in the face of proliferating threats and the increasing complexity introduced by AI. As a result, there is no technical breakdown of a vulnerability or exploit, affected systems or versions, or specific remediation steps. However, the article highlights the growing demand for cybersecurity expertise, even on a part-time basis, indicating that companies are recognizing the importance of cybersecurity in the face of evolving threats. CISOs are reportedly finding the job harder due to the increasing complexity, but the article does not provide any specific actionable advice for defenders. Overall, this is more of a trend or awareness item rather than a specific threat. Despite this, the increasing complexity and demand for cybersecurity expertise may lead to a shortage of skilled professionals, making it a medium-severity issue for the industry as a whole.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1056| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1068| C[Discovery]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

And yet, breach dwell times remain stubbornly long (~43 days), response windows keep closing before teams can act, and analysts burn out triaging noise instead

items.title: Apple Patches Beats Studio Buds Flaw Letting Nearby Attackers Spy via Microphone
items.description: Apple has updated its Beats Studio Buds wireless earbuds to patch a high-severity vulnerability that could be exploited by nearby hackers to eavesdrop on users. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2025-20701 (CVSS score: 8.8), refers to a case of incorrect authorization impacting the Airoha Bluetooth audio SDK that makes it possible to pair a Bluetooth audio device without user consent.

items.title: Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure
items.description: CISA has given federal agencies only three days to patch CVE-2026-20253, which can be exploited for unauthenticated remote code execution. The post Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Gentlemen ransomware uses multiple EDR killers to disable defenses
items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) is actively developing and maintaining a suite of endpoint detection and response (EDR) killers to help affiliates evade detection in attacks. [...]

items.title: Novo Nordisk Breach Exposes Software Development Pipeline Risk
items.description: A leaked GitHub token underscores what most organizations get wrong: Treating secrets management as a tooling problem rather than an identity problem.

items.title: Bulgaria allowed surveillance tech firm to sell products to repressive regimes, report says
items.description: The nonprofit Human Rights Watch obtained export licensing records covering 2018 through 2023, which show the Bulgarian government allowed the surveillance firm Circles to peddle the tech to law enforcement and intelligence agencies in several countries known for human rights abuses.

items.title: Operation Escaneo Signals Shift in LatAm Threat Landscape
items.description: The threat group's curious business model may combine opportunistic monetization alongside intel collection, without much coordination between the two.

items.title: Nintendo confirms data stolen in WebMD subsidiary cyberattack
items.description: Nintendo of America has confirmed to BleepingComputer that threat actors stole survey data from the third-party TinyPulse service used internally, but its systems were not compromised. [...]

items.title: FIFA Bug Exposes World Cup Streams to Remote Takeover
items.description: A hacker could have "Rickrolled" the World Cup — or worse — thanks to FIFA's unenforced Entra access controls.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: F5 Patches Two Critical NGINX Open Source Flaws Enabling Remote Code Execution
items.description: F5 has released security updates to address two critical security flaws in NGINX Open Source that could be exploited to achieve code execution on affected systems. The vulnerabilities are listed below - CVE-2026-42530 (CVSS v4 score: 9.2) - A use-after-free vulnerability in the ngx_http_v3_module that could be triggered by a remote unauthenticated attacker when NGINX Open Source is

items.title: Majority of Internet-Accessible REDCap Servers Outdated
items.description: These servers are regularly targeted by China-linked UNC6508 for initial access and backdoor deployment. The post Majority of Internet-Accessible REDCap Servers Outdated appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Salesforce Data Thefts Continue via Klue App Compromise
items.description: Klue's Battlecards is now the third integrated application that has been compromised to steal customers' Salesforce data, and victims include Huntress, the cybersecurity vendor.

items.title: USB worm spreads crypto-stealing malware via Windows shortcut files
items.description: Threat actors targeting cryptocurrency wallets have been distributing clipboard-stealing malware with self-spreading capabilities and using the Tor network to conceal communication. [...]

items.title: Orphaned AI Agents: How to Find Hidden Access Risks Inside Your Network
items.description: If an autonomous AI agent interacts with your company's core intellectual property today, can your security team instantly name the person who authorized it? For most enterprises, the answer is a simple no. The rush to adopt internal AI tools has left a massive trail of administrative debt: orphaned agents (AI tools left running after their creator leaves the company) and standing privileges (

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: Claude Chat Abuse, NastyC2 npm Packages, Device-Code Phishing + 25 More Stories
items.description: The internet did not break this week. It got used exactly as designed, which is worse. Searches were siphoned through shady browser add-ons. AI chat links turned into malware delivery paths. macOS attacks ran in memory and left almost nothing behind. Cloud agents looked like helpers until attackers treated them like open shells. Add exposed edge gear, poisoned packages, cash courier scams,

items.title: Microsoft Details Windows Clipper Malware Campaign Using USB LNK Worm and Tor-Based C2
items.description: Microsoft has disclosed details of a Windows-based cryptocurrency clipper campaign that has targeted users since February 2026 with clipboard-intercepting malware with self-spreading capabilities and using the Tor anonymity network to hide communication. "The clipper in this campaign relies on Windows Script Host and ActiveX-driven logic to launch a bundled Tor proxy and poll a hidden-service C2

items.title: Australian sugar producer works to restore operations as ransomware group claims attack
items.description: Mackay Sugar said it was "working urgently" to verify claims that a highly active ransomware group was behind a cyberattack that shut down harvesting and milling operations.

items.title: Klue OAuth breach linked to 'Icarus' Salesforce data theft attacks
items.description: Market intelligence platform Klue suffered a OAuth breach that enabled the "Icarus" threat actors to steal Salesforce CRM data from multiple organizations in an ongoing extortion campaign. [...]

items.title: Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push
items.description: The deal values industrial cybersecurity giant Dragos at $3.25 billion, and runZero and NetRise will operate under Dragos. The post Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Get Out of Security Debt by Tackling the Exposure Problem
items.description: Teams digging out of security debt need to answer only two simple questions: Which vulnerabilities in our systems are exposed, and how long should they stay that way?

items.title: No Exploits Required
items.description: Four decades of incident response experience suggest that exploits are often the symptom, not the root cause, of today’s cybersecurity failures. The post No Exploits Required appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: EU Gets a Head Start in Developing 6G Network Security
items.description: "Shield-6G" will combine AI threat detection, digital twins, honeypots, and more, to help carriers protect 6G networks against the threats of tomorrow.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 18th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9978, (Thu, Jun 18th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: INC Ransomware Thrives by Mastering the Basics
items.description: And one of those basics is focusing on sectors where a ransomware disruption creates immediate pressure to pay up, like with healthcare.

items.title: Hostile states behind three-quarters of attacks on Britain's critical infrastructure, cyber chief warns
items.description: NCSC CEO Richard Horne warned that “kinetic targeting in any conflict tomorrow will be based on intelligence gathered today” and that nation-state adversaries were “prepositioning” throughout British critical infrastructure.

items.title: The browser blind spot: Why your security tool may not be blocking what you think it is [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a guest diary submitted by Varun Murdula]

items.title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises 30K+ Fortinet Devices
items.description: Attackers are actively targeting various sectors across nearly 200 countries and already have compiled a list of working credentials for tens of thousands of compromised devices.

items.title: UK Social Media Ban for Minors Has Privacy Experts Worried
items.description: The UK will ban adolescents under 16 years old from user-to-user social media platforms, despite age verification issues and privacy concerns.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 17th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9976, (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.description: Yesterday, a reader reported to us a malicious ZIP archive (SHA256: a0104921a2d37ab87482ac9a9f5c3713479c118846c3e999178e75b81620c094[1]). Once unzipped, it contains a VHDX file that discloses a malicious JavaScript after being mounted (which is automatic on modern Windows OSs):

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Evil MSI Background: BASE64 Statistical Analysis, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: I like it when a fellow handler posts a diary entry about images with malicious content. Last one is Xavier: "The Evil MSI Background is Back!".

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 15th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9972, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, June 12th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9970, (Fri, Jun 12th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
